subject_profile.summary: Base country-level assessment; default category weights prioritize rule of law, institutional integrity, and personal freedom.
stated_values: Rule of law and institutional integrity weighted most heavily
Personal freedom and security from arbitrary state action are foundational
Economic success is contingent on rule of law, not a substitute for it

flags: Generated 2026-05-28 under v2.0. STATE-MEDIA EXCLUSION applied: UAE state-aligned outlets (WAM, The National, Khaleej Times, Gulf News) were excluded on governance/rights/politics and rebuilt from independent sources (Freedom House, RSF, HRW, V-Dem, World Justice Project, The Sentry, UN panels); economic data used only where IMF-validated.

The core tension is world-class capacity, safety, infrastructure, and easy tax-free residency against an authoritarian floor: no political freedom, very low press freedom, and no path to belonging for the ~88% expatriate population.
Resource/rentier dynamic: oil wealth is partly offset by sovereign funds and diversification, but the long-term economic score is discounted for it.

Recalibrated 2026-05-29 for the 2026 Iran war (Operation Epic Fury, US+Israel vs Iran from 28 Feb) and the Strait of Hormuz closure: blockade of ~1/5 of world oil+LNG, attacks/threats on Gulf desalination (water security), a fragile/violated 8 Apr ceasefire, and the global energy shock. (Reuters; BBC; NYT; Al Jazeera; CSIS; Wikipedia)

economic.notes: Deep sovereign-wealth buffers, dollar-pegged stability, and a diversified hub economy; an oil-dependent rentier tail on the long horizon.

fiscal_state.notes: Diversified, fund-backed finances absorb the shock better than peers, but Hormuz disruption and war risk dent the near term. (IMF)

monetary_independence.notes: The dirham is hard-pegged to the US dollar, so there is essentially no independent monetary policy (US rates are imported); the peg is deliberate and delivers convertibility and stability. (IMF Art IV 2025)

reserve_currency_intl_monetary.notes: Not an issuer, but dirham stability rides the USD peg - a mild positive for holders. (IMF)

banking_stability.notes: Well-capitalised (CAR ~17%), low NPLs, ample liquidity; the IMF judges stability risks contained. (IMF GFSR 2026; CBUAE)

trade_actions_capacity.notes: The Habshan-Fujairah pipeline (~1.5 mbd) lands some crude on the Gulf of Oman outside Hormuz, but most trade and re-export flows still depend on the blockaded strait. Partial bypass, large near-term hit. (Reuters; EIA)

institutional.notes: High state capacity and functional commercial law, against no judicial independence on political matters and among the world's lowest press freedom.

rule_of_law.notes: A split: commercial/contract law is functional and modernizing (the DIFC/ADGM English-law courts), but there is no judicial independence on political matters and grave due-process violations in political cases. (WJP 2025; Freedom House 2025)

statistical_integrity.notes: Economic statistics are IMF-validated and usable; political and rights data are opaque, with no independent civil society. (IMF Art IV 2025)

civil_service_capacity.notes: Very high state capacity and execution (infrastructure delivery, COP28, an AI/G42 build-out). (IMF; observed)

press_freedom.notes: No independent media; RSF ranks the UAE very low (~164); censorship and cybercrime laws, surveillance, and journalist persecution. (RSF 2025; HRW)

political_social.notes: Suppressed/managed regime stability and easy entry, against sharply restricted civil liberties and no path to belonging for the expatriate majority.

political_stability.notes: Suppressed/managed stability: high near-term regime control via autocracy rather than pluralist resilience; the long-term is discounted for hereditary-rule brittleness and no political release valve. (Freedom House 2025; V-Dem)

civil_liberties.notes: Rated 'Not Free': banned parties and unions, criminalized dissent, surveillance, and kafala-legacy labour abuses (wage theft, passport confiscation). (Freedom House 2025; HRW)

treatment_non_citizens.notes: Easy lawful ENTRY - a 10-year Golden Visa, remote-work visas, easy sponsorship, no income tax - but a hard ceiling: expatriates are ~88% of the population with no path to citizenship or political rights, and ordinary residency is tied to employment, creating structural precarity. (Freedom House; HRW; UAE visa rules)

social_cohesion.notes: Orderly, very low crime, and cosmopolitan, but an expatriate-majority society with no rights and a stratified labour underclass caps it. (Gallup; HRW)

demographics.notes: A young, expat-driven, favourable working-age structure near-term, discounted long-term for structural dependence on continuously imported labour (no organic citizen base). (UN; IMF)

geopolitical.notes: A reliable-but-non-aligned regional power with active conflict exposure.

alliance_reliability.notes: A US Major Defense Partner and regional power, but actively hedging with China and Russia - reliable but non-aligned. (Washington Institute)

conflict_involvement.notes: Targeted by Iranian strikes during the 2026 war; deeply exposed as a Gulf trade and finance hub. (Al Jazeera)

sanctions_capital_controls.notes: An open capital account (a positive for holders), but ongoing sanctions-evasion and illicit-finance scrutiny despite removal from the FATF grey list in 2024. (FATF; The Sentry)

physical_practical.notes: World-class infrastructure, very low crime, and excellent hub healthcare, against extreme heat and water/desalination dependence.

climate.notes: Extreme wet-bulb heat by mid-century, desal-dependent water, coastal/sea-level, with the highest-readiness Gulf offset near-term. (IPCC AR6; ND-GAIN)

healthcare.notes: Excellent modern private/hub healthcare, of medical-tourism grade. (IMF; OSAC)

infrastructure.notes: Among the best infrastructure on the board, but heavy desalination dependence under direct Iranian water-infrastructure threats is an acute near-term vulnerability. (CSIS; Circle of Blue)

crime_safety.notes: A genuine strength: very low crime; surveys show ~95% feel safe at night. (Gallup)

disaster_insurance.notes: Flood exposure newly realized after the 2024 floods; the insurance market is developing, with high state response capacity. (2024 floods coverage)

summary: World-class infrastructure, very low crime, excellent hub healthcare, deep fiscal buffers from sovereign wealth, dollar-pegged monetary stability, and uniquely easy, tax-free residency for professionals. The offsetting floor is authoritarian: no judicial independence on political matters, among the world's lowest press freedom, sharply restricted civil liberties, and no path to citizenship or political rights for the ~88% expatriate population (residency tied to employment). Active regional-conflict exposure and severe long-run climate/water stress add risk, and the economy carries a rentier, oil-dependent tail.
